text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY CONSULTATION NOTE
Post-Cytoreductive Nephrectomy Assessment -- Initiation of Systemic Therapy

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 63-year-old retired industrial engineer presenting for post-operative oncology assessment and initiation of systemic immunotherapy. He is approximately 4 weeks out from cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection. He was referred by his urologic surgeon, Dr. [redacted], for integration into ongoing oncologic management.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski initially presented several weeks prior with a 3-week history of gross hematuria, right flank pain, and approximately 15 pounds of unintentional weight loss over 3 months. Cross-sectional imaging at that time demonstrated a large heterogeneous right renal mass measuring 11.4 x 9.8 x 10.1 cm arising from the upper and mid pole of the right kidney, with central necrosis and extension of tumor thrombus into the right renal vein (confirmed on MRI to not involve the IVC). CT staging revealed multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules consistent with metastatic disease (largest 1.4 cm in the right lower lobe) as well as enlarged retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy including a 2.8 cm right para-aortic node and a 1.9 cm aortocaval node.

CT-guided core needle biopsy confirmed clear cell renal cell carcinoma, WHO/ISUP grade 4, with sarcomatoid differentiation comprising approximately 20% of the sampled tissue. PD-L1 by SP142 showed a combined positive score of 15 (tumor proportion score 10%). Comprehensive genomic profiling via FoundationOne CDx was notable for VHL p.L89fs frameshift deletion, PBRM1 nonsense mutation, and BAP1 nonsense mutation -- the co-occurrence of PBRM1 and BAP1 loss being a biologically significant finding associated with higher-grade disease and sarcomatoid dedifferentiation. Additional alterations included SETD2 loss-of-function, KDM5C loss-of-function, CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion, MET amplification (copy number 6.8), TP53 p.R248W, and a TERT promoter mutation. Chromosome 3p loss encompassing all major VHL pathway loci was also identified. Tumor mutational burden was low (6.2 mutations/Mb), microsatellite stable. PD-L1 by 22C3 showed a CPS of 18.

The case was reviewed by our multidisciplinary tumor board, which included urologic surgery, medical oncology, radiation oncology, and diagnostic radiology. Given his relatively limited pulmonary metastatic burden, resectable primary tumor with renal vein thrombus, and symptoms attributable to the primary mass, the consensus recommendation was cytoreductive nephrectomy followed by systemic immunotherapy-based combination therapy. The patient was deemed IMDC intermediate risk (two adverse factors: time from diagnosis to systemic therapy less than one year, and thrombocytosis with platelet count of 478,000/uL at presentation).

He subsequently underwent open right radical nephrectomy with en bloc excision of Gerota fascia, right adrenalectomy, and template retroperitoneal lymph node dissection. Renal vein thrombus was excised. Estimated blood loss was 950 mL; two units of packed red blood cells were transfused intraoperatively. Operative time was 4.5 hours. Final surgical pathology confirmed clear cell RCC, WHO/ISUP grade 4, with sarcomatoid differentiation revised to 22% of viable tumor volume. The tumor measured 11.6 x 10.2 x 9.7 cm. Lymphovascular invasion was present. Renal sinus and perinephric fat invasion were both identified. Three of eight retroperitoneal lymph nodes were positive for metastatic clear cell RCC with sarcomatoid features. All surgical margins were negative (R0). The right adrenal gland was not involved. PD-L1 in the sarcomatoid areas (22C3) was notably high with a CPS of 22. Pathologic stage assigned as ypT3bN1 in the setting of residual distant metastatic disease.

Today, approximately 4 weeks following surgery, the patient presents in follow-up. He reports that his recovery has been overall unremarkable. The gross hematuria that prompted his initial presentation has completely resolved. His right flank pain has substantially improved and he no longer requires narcotic analgesia. He is ambulating well, tolerating a regular diet, and performing activities of daily living without significant restriction. He notes some mild fatigue, which he attributes to the surgery and his known mild anemia. He denies fever, chills, wound drainage, shortness of breath, chest pain, palpitations, or new neurological symptoms.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

1. Metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma, right kidney, WHO/ISUP grade 4, with sarcomatoid differentiation (22%), stage IV (cT3bN1M1 at diagnosis; ypT3bN1 post-cytoreduction; residual bilateral pulmonary and retroperitoneal nodal metastases) -- diagnosed approximately 8 weeks ago
2. Hypertension -- longstanding, previously well-controlled on lisinopril
3. Type 2 diabetes mellitus -- managed with metformin
4. Former smoker -- 30 pack-year history, quit 15 years ago
5. Mild chronic kidney disease (CKD stage IIIa, creatinine 1.1 mg/dL pre-operatively; now expected to have reduced GFR post-nephrectomy)
6. Intraoperative anemia requiring transfusion -- 2 units pRBC during nephrectomy

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY

1. Cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection, adrenalectomy -- approximately 4 weeks ago
2. No other prior surgical history reported

FAMILY HISTORY

No family history of renal cell carcinoma, bladder cancer, or other urologic malignancies reported. Father with coronary artery disease; mother with type 2 diabetes. No known hereditary cancer syndromes (no family history suggestive of von Hippel-Lindau, Birt-Hogg-Dube, hereditary leiomyomatosis and renal cell cancer, or SDH-deficient RCC syndromes). Two adult children in good health.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Mr. Kowalski is a retired industrial engineer. He is married and lives with his wife. He reports a 30 pack-year smoking history (1 pack per day for 30 years) and quit 15 years ago. Denies current tobacco use. Drinks alcohol occasionally, approximately 2-4 standard drinks per week, no history of heavy alcohol use or liver disease. Denies illicit drug use. He was previously quite active but has been limited since his diagnosis; he hopes to return to light exercise (walking, gardening) as he recovers. He has strong family and social support. He and his wife have been engaged and ask appropriate questions. He expresses a clear understanding of his diagnosis and its implications.

ALLERGIES

No known drug allergies. No known contrast dye allergy (received IV contrast without issue during staging CT and MRI).

MEDICATIONS (current)

1. Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily (hypertension)
2. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily with meals (type 2 diabetes)
3. Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg orally as needed (post-operative pain, using sparingly)
4. Multivitamin daily
5. Ferrous sulfate 325 mg orally once daily (initiated post-operatively for anemia; ongoing)

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Mild fatigue present (grade 1), gradually improving since surgery. No fevers, no chills. No new weight loss (weight is stable at approximately 183 lbs today; he had lost 15 lbs prior to diagnosis). Appetite improving.
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: No headache, no visual changes, no hearing changes, no epistaxis.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations. Mild bilateral lower extremity swelling noted by patient, improving since discharge. No syncope.
Respiratory: Mild dyspnea on exertion with stairs (attributed to post-operative deconditioning and anemia); no hemoptysis, no pleuritic chest pain.
Gastrointestinal: Bowel function restored post-operatively. No nausea, no vomiting. Mild constipation resolving. No melena or hematochezia.
Genitourinary: Hematuria fully resolved. No dysuria. Normal urinary stream from remaining left kidney.
Musculoskeletal: Incisional soreness at laparotomy wound site, improving. No new bone pain, no back pain beyond expected post-operative discomfort.
Neurological: No new numbness, weakness, or neurological deficits.
Skin: Surgical wound healing well per patient report and surgeon documentation.
Endocrine: No symptoms of hyperglycemia or hypoglycemia. Blood sugars reportedly well-controlled on metformin.
Psychiatric: Patient is anxious regarding his diagnosis and upcoming treatment but reports coping well with support from his wife and close friends. No depression or suicidal ideation.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 136/84 mmHg (well-controlled), heart rate 72 beats per minute (regular), respiratory rate 16 breaths per minute, temperature 37.0 degrees Celsius (afebrile), oxygen saturation 97% on room air, weight 183 lbs (83 kg), height 5'11" (180 cm), BMI 25.7 kg/m2.

General: Mr. Kowalski is a well-appearing, well-nourished male in no acute distress. He is alert and oriented times three. He appears slightly fatigued but is in good spirits. He arrived independently to the appointment.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Conjunctivae pale but not icteric; sclerae white. Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light bilaterally. No lymphadenopathy in the cervical, submandibular, or supraclavicular regions. Oropharynx clear. No oral mucosal lesions.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. Trace bilateral lower extremity pitting edema (1+), improved per patient.

Respiratory: Chest wall expansion symmetric. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No dullness to percussion. No crackles or wheeze. Respiratory effort is comfortable at rest.

Abdomen: Midline laparotomy incision is clean, dry, and intact. Staples are in place with no erythema, dehiscence, or signs of infection. The abdomen is soft. There is mild tenderness to deep palpation in the right upper quadrant and right flank, expected post-operatively. No rebound tenderness. Bowel sounds present in all quadrants. The liver and spleen are not palpably enlarged. No palpable abdominal or pelvic masses appreciated.

Back/Flank: No costovertebral angle tenderness. Incision extending into the right flank is healing well.

Extremities: No cyanosis or clubbing. Trace bilateral lower extremity edema as above. No deep vein thrombosis concern clinically; patient has been ambulatory.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II through XII grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in all four extremities. Sensation grossly intact to light touch. Coordination intact. No focal neurological deficits.

Skin: Laparotomy wound healing well. No rash. No skin lesions suggestive of paraneoplastic manifestations.

Lymph Nodes: No palpable axillary, inguinal, or pelvic lymphadenopathy on examination. Post-lymphadenectomy changes in the retroperitoneal/para-aortic region not assessable clinically.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 7.8 x 10^3/uL (normal; no leukocytosis or leukopenia)
- Hemoglobin: 10.8 g/dL (mild anemia, improved from 10.2 g/dL at diagnosis; recovering from intraoperative blood loss on iron supplementation)
- Hematocrit: 32.4%
- Platelets: 398,000/uL (down from 478,000/uL; paraneoplastic thrombocytosis improving after cytoreductive surgery)
- Neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio: 3.1 (improved from 4.2 at diagnosis)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Creatinine: 1.6 mg/dL (expected increase from pre-operative baseline of 1.1 mg/dL; patient now has only one functional kidney; GFR estimated at 46 mL/min/1.73m2)
- BUN: 22 mg/dL
- Sodium: 139 mEq/L
- Potassium: 4.2 mEq/L
- Chloride: 102 mEq/L
- Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
- Glucose: 114 mg/dL (mildly elevated; consistent with known T2DM; not significantly worse than prior)
- Calcium: 9.8 mg/dL (normalized from pre-operative 10.9 mg/dL; paraneoplastic hypercalcemia resolved after cytoreduction)
- Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL
- AST: 24 U/L (normal)
- ALT: 28 U/L (normal)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 78 U/L (normal)
- Albumin: 3.7 g/dL (mildly low, likely nutritional; improving)
- Total protein: 6.9 g/dL

LDH: 198 U/L (improved from 287 U/L at diagnosis after cytoreduction)

Thyroid Function Tests: TSH 2.1 mIU/L (normal, baseline prior to immunotherapy)

Hemoglobin A1c: 7.1% (modestly elevated; consistent with known T2DM; acceptable for oncologic management context; will co-manage with PCP)

Urinalysis: Clear, no hematuria (confirmed), no proteinuria, trace glucose.

Coagulation studies: PT 11.8 sec, INR 1.0 (normal), aPTT 28 sec (normal).

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Most recent imaging reviewed: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast from the time of staging. Post-cytoreductive nephrectomy, the primary renal mass and associated renal vein thrombus have been surgically resected. Residual known metastatic disease includes:
- Bilateral pulmonary nodules: right lower lobe 1.4 cm, left lower lobe 0.8 cm, right upper lobe 0.6 cm
- Retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy: 2.8 cm right para-aortic node, 1.9 cm aortocaval node (nodes were partially addressed by lymphadenectomy; residual disease may remain)

No new imaging has been obtained in the immediate post-operative period beyond routine clinical assessment. Baseline restaging CT will be obtained approximately 3 months after initiating systemic therapy per standard practice.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Kowalski is a 63-year-old male with metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma, WHO/ISUP grade 4, with significant sarcomatoid differentiation (22%), IMDC intermediate risk (2 factors), who is now 4 weeks post cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy and presenting for initiation of systemic immunotherapy. His post-operative recovery has been unremarkable with resolution of presenting symptoms (hematuria, flank pain), normalization of his paraneoplastic hypercalcemia and thrombocytosis, and adequate performance status.

1. METASTATIC CLEAR CELL RENAL CELL CARCINOMA -- SYSTEMIC THERAPY INITIATION

The multidisciplinary tumor board recommendation, which I endorse, is to proceed with first-line combination immunotherapy with ipilimumab (Yervoy) 1 mg/kg plus nivolumab (Opdivo) 3 mg/kg intravenously every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles, followed by nivolumab 480 mg flat dose IV every 4 weeks as maintenance therapy.

Rationale for this regimen:
- CheckMate-214 established ipilimumab plus nivolumab as a standard first-line option for metastatic clear cell RCC, with superior overall survival compared to sunitinib in intermediate- and poor-risk patients. The 5-year follow-up data continue to show a meaningful survival tail in approximately 25-30% of patients, suggesting potential for durable, long-term disease control.
- His tumor's pathologic and molecular profile strongly supports immunotherapy: WHO/ISUP grade 4 disease with sarcomatoid differentiation is known to be highly enriched for PD-L1 expression and has demonstrated particularly robust responses to dual checkpoint blockade -- a prospective analysis within CheckMate-214 showed an objective response rate of approximately 56% in sarcomatoid RCC subsets with ipi/nivo, compared to approximately 19% with sunitinib. His PD-L1 CPS of 18-22 (depending on assay used) further supports anticipated immunotherapy sensitivity.
- BAP1 loss, identified on his NGS, has been associated with higher-grade clear cell RCC and sarcomatoid features; although no specific approved targeted therapy exists for BAP1-deficient RCC, the association with immune-rich tumor biology further supports checkpoint inhibitor-based therapy.
- MET amplification (copy number 6.8) on NGS is noted -- this may be relevant for second-line TKI selection if progression were to occur, as cabozantinib has potent activity against both VEGFR and MET. This will be kept in mind for future treatment planning.
- IMDC intermediate risk patients derive meaningful benefit from ipi/nivo and represent the core population from CheckMate-214.

Treatment plan details:
- Ipilimumab 1 mg/kg IV over 30 minutes, immediately followed by nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV over 30 minutes, every 21 days x 4 cycles (induction)
- Following cycle 4: nivolumab 480 mg IV flat dose every 28 days (maintenance monotherapy)
- Premedications: diphenhydramine 25 mg IV and acetaminophen 650 mg PO prior to each infusion
- Labs prior to each cycle: CBC with differential, CMP (including LFTs, creatinine, electrolytes), TSH every other cycle
- Baseline TSH documented today as 2.1 mIU/L (normal)
- Echocardiogram not routinely required but will be ordered if cardiac symptoms develop

First cycle scheduled to be administered in the infusion center this week.

2. IMMUNE-RELATED ADVERSE EVENT (irAE) COUNSELING

The patient and his wife received extensive counseling today regarding the spectrum of immune-related adverse events that can occur with dual PD-1/CTLA-4 blockade. Key points covered:

- Endocrinopathies: hypothyroidism (most common), hyperthyroidism (less common, can precede hypothyroidism), hypophysitis (pituitary inflammation -- can cause fatigue, headache, and hormonal deficiencies), adrenal insufficiency (potentially life-threatening -- must report severe fatigue, nausea, hypotension), type 1 diabetes (rare)
- Colitis: immune-mediated diarrhea/colitis is one of the most common grade 3/4 events with CTLA-4 blockade; patient instructed to report any significant increase in bowel frequency, abdominal cramping, or bloody stool immediately
- Hepatitis: asymptomatic transaminase elevation most common; LFTs monitored at each cycle
- Pneumonitis: immune-mediated inflammation of the lung; report any new or worsening shortness of breath, cough, or hypoxia -- particularly relevant given his pulmonary metastases making clinical monitoring more challenging
- Dermatitis: rash is common; usually manageable; severe blistering or mucous membrane involvement requires prompt attention
- Nephritis: report decreased urine output or significant edema (also need to monitor creatinine closely given post-nephrectomy CKD)
- Neurotoxicity: rare but serious; report any new weakness, numbness, double vision, or cognitive changes
- Myocarditis: rare but potentially life-threatening; report chest pain, palpitations, or syncope immediately
- Arthritis/myositis: joint pains and muscle inflammation can occur

Patient given written irAE information sheet and oncology nurse contact phone number for urgent concerns. He verbalized understanding of when to seek immediate medical attention versus routine reporting at next visit. Instructed NOT to self-administer steroids or stop treatment without contacting our team.

In general, grade 1-2 endocrinopathies are managed with hormone replacement and continuation of therapy; grade 2 colitis, pneumonitis, or hepatitis typically requires treatment hold and oral steroids; grade 3-4 events require high-dose IV steroids and likely permanent discontinuation of the offending agent. Given his dual combination therapy, the rate of grade 3/4 irAEs is approximately 25-35% vs. ~17% with single-agent nivolumab.

3. POST-NEPHRECTOMY RENAL FUNCTION

Creatinine of 1.6 mg/dL (eGFR approximately 46 mL/min/1.73m2) represents expected post-nephrectomy decline from his pre-operative baseline of 1.1 mg/dL. This is consistent with normal physiologic adaptation to unilateral nephrectomy. His creatinine should stabilize or modestly improve over the next several months as hypertrophic adaptation of the remaining left kidney occurs. Checkpoint inhibitors are generally well-tolerated in patients with CKD; however, immune-mediated nephritis (tubulointerstitial nephritis) is a recognized irAE that would need to be distinguished from progressive CKD. Baseline creatinine documented today. Metformin continued at current dose (eGFR > 45 mL/min/1.73m2); will reassess if creatinine worsens. Lisinopril continued for both hypertension and renoprotection; blood pressure is well-controlled at 136/84 today.

4. ANEMIA

Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL, recovering from post-operative blood loss. Ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily continued. Reticulocyte count not checked today but anemia is consistent with post-surgical etiology. Will recheck CBC at cycle 2. Transfusion threshold approximately 7-8 g/dL unless symptomatic. Patient has mild exertional dyspnea consistent with anemia and deconditioning; this should improve with recovery.

5. DIABETES MANAGEMENT

HbA1c 7.1% -- acceptable management. Glucose 114 mg/dL today. Metformin continued as above. Particular vigilance will be maintained for immune checkpoint inhibitor-associated type 1 diabetes (very rare but can present as fulminant DKA). Patient instructed to report markedly elevated blood sugars, excessive thirst, polyuria, nausea, or vomiting. Blood glucose monitoring advised at home during treatment.

6. HYPERTENSION

BP 136/84 mmHg today -- well-controlled on lisinopril 10 mg daily. No medication changes at this time.

7. HYPOTHYROIDISM SCREENING

Baseline TSH normal. Thyroid function will be checked every other cycle (every 6 weeks during induction). Patient counseled that hypothyroidism is the most common endocrine irAE with this regimen and is readily managed with levothyroxine replacement, which does not require dose modification or treatment interruption.

8. PERFORMANCE STATUS

KPS 80, ECOG 1. Patient is ambulatory and capable of all self-care. Recovering well from major abdominal surgery. No contraindication to initiating systemic therapy at this time. He will be reassessed at each cycle.

9. FOLLOW-UP AND MONITORING

- Cycle 1 of ipilimumab 1 mg/kg + nivolumab 3 mg/kg to be administered in infusion center this week
- Labs prior to each cycle (CBC, CMP, LFTs, TSH q2 cycles)
- Telephone check-in by oncology nurse at 1 week after first infusion to assess for early irAEs
- Next clinic visit in 3 weeks (prior to cycle 2)
- Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis after completing 4 induction cycles (approximately 3 months from today)
- PCP to co-manage diabetes and hypertension; communication sent today
- Referral to oncology nutrition and survivorship clinic placed

Time spent with patient and family today: approximately 65 minutes, predominantly in counseling, education, and discussion of the treatment plan, risks, benefits, and alternatives.

Signing Oncologist: [Attending Medical Oncologist, Genitourinary Oncology Program]
Department: Medical Oncology -- Genitourinary Oncology
